raw_case:
Dural arteriovenous fistula mimicking a stroke: A misdiagnosis of two months
We present a case of a 70-year-old male who presented with left-sided weakness and dysarthria. Cranial imaging was suggestive of a cerebellar infarct and the patient was treated with aspirin and clopidogrel. Two months later a fall prompted further cranial imaging, which was concerning for an intracranial mass with vasogenic edema. Computed tomography angiogram (CTA) was negative for vascular lesion. Ultimately, a DSA revealed a Borden III dAVF between the right occipital artery and the posterior cerebellar vein that was treated with endovascular embolization.

Introduction

Dural arteriovenous fistulas (dAVF) are an uncommon entity that involve arteriovenous shunting between meningeal arteries and the dural venous sinuses or veins. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) is the gold standard for diagnosis [1]. Presenting symptoms vary and are largely dependent on location [2]. Imaging findings, particularly on a typical preliminary work-up with head computed tomography (CT) may be nonspecific [3]. Misdiagnosis of dAVF may occur and has been reported to mimic brain tumor tumor [[4], [5], [6]], encephalitis [3], dementia [[7], [8], [9]], venous sinus thrombosis [10], and stroke [[11], [12], [13]].We report a case of a patient who was misdiagnosed and treated for a cerebellar stroke for several months, then nearly misdiagnosed with a cerebellar mass, prior to a DSA demonstrating the true pathology of a cerebellar dAVF.

Case presentation

A 70-year-old male with past medical history of coronary artery disease, prostate cancer with prior prostatectomy, colon cancer with prior sigmoid colon resection, hypertension, and previous smoking history presented to an outside emergency department after a fall. He was found to have a right-sided hip fracture and underwent right hip arthroplasty. Upon discharge from the hospital, he was admitted to a skilled nursing facility. On postoperative day ten, he returned to the emergency department for left-sided weakness and dysarthria. Head CT (computed tomography) demonstrated hypodensity within the right cerebellum, causing mass effect on the fourth ventricle. Computed tomography angiogram (CTA) was also performed. Images were unavailable for independent review, but the medical record indicated that the radiologist reported the images were negative for vascular lesion. The report suggested a “probable acute infarction in the right cerebellum.”An MRI (magnetic resonance image) was subsequently performed that showed an area of hyperintensity on fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) MRI, causing mass effect on the fourth ventricle with associated hydrocephalus (Fig. 1). Postcontrast images showed heterogenous enhancement without a discrete lesion (Fig. 2). Records from the outside facility indicate that the patient was believed to have a cerebellar infarct. An EVD (external ventricular drain) was placed and removed 2 days later. The patient was started on aspirin and clopidogrel for a presumed stroke. He was discharged to skilled nursing facility due to persistent left-sided weakness.Two months later, the patient had another fall and was taken to our emergency department. He reported left hip pain, but due to the fall he also underwent a head CT. Hip radiograph demonstrated a left hip fracture. Head CT again demonstrated a hypodensity within the right cerebellum with associated edema (Fig. 3). Given the degree of unresolved edema, our team was consulted for possible brain mass. MRI brain showed worsening edema with near effacement of the fourth ventricle (Fig. 4). A CTA was negative for vascular lesion or occlusion. Given his presumed history of stroke, the patient underwent an echocardiogram which showed normal ejection fracture.At this point the patient was at his reported new neurological baseline according to his wife, with left-sided weakness and dysarthria. Due to the edema, dexamethasone 4 milligrams every 8 hours was initiated. The next day the patient underwent left hip arthroplasty.Once the fracture was stabilized, a multidisciplinary discussion occurred between the medical team, neurology, radiology, interventional radiology, and neurosurgery. With dexamethasone, the patient had subjective improvement of his symptoms. At this point, the presumed diagnosis of stroke 2 months prior was questioned. Concern was raised that perhaps an underlying mass had been the culprit all along. Neurosurgery was urged to perform a biopsy for further diagnosis. However, given the concern for an underlying vascular lesion, further discussion suggested a digital subtraction angiogram (DSA) as the next step.On postoperative day 2 from the left hip arthroplasty, the patient underwent DSA. The procedure revealed a right posterior, paramedian cerebellar arteriovenous fistula between the branches of the right occipital artery and posterior cerebellar vein (Figs. 5A and B), as well as severe stenosis of the right vertebral artery at its origin.The patient was continued on dexamethasone 4 milligrams every 8 hours. On postprocedure day eight, he underwent repeat DSA with plans for embolization. Diminutive shunt vascularity related to the distal right occipital artery with slow flow into a venous pouch was identified. The right posterior occipital artery was embolized; complete disconnection was visualized after embolization (Figs. 5C and D).After embolization, the patient was weaned off dexamethasone. He was discharged on postprocedure day 5 from the embolization to skilled nursing facility. Upon discharge he had persistent left-sided weakness but was otherwise neurologically intact.

Discussion

Dural AVFs compose approximately 10%-15% of intracranial arteriovenous malformations [11]. They can be difficult to diagnose due to the nonspecific presentation and findings on noninvasive imaging [7]. The exact etiology of dural AVFs remains unclear, but is believed for be associated with venous hypertension [12]. Symptoms vary depending on location, but the hallmark presentation is pulsatile tinnitus due to the increased shunting of blood into the dural venous sinuses [14]. Our patient presented with hemiparesis and dysarthria, symptoms mimicking an ischemic stroke.Three previous reports have described dAVF mimicking stroke [[11], [12], [13]]. However, all had findings on noninvasive imaging suggestive of dAVF that altered the treatment course. None of them were misdiagnosed and anticoagulated for several months, as our patient was prior to presenting to our institution.One case described an 80-year-old female who presented with “stroke-like symptoms” of confusion, hemiparesis, and aphasia. However, imaging revealed a vascular malformation and the patient was not treated as an ischemic stroke [11].Another report consistent with stroke on presentation involved a 76-year-old man who presents with aphasia [12]. Initial head CT was negative for hemorrhage and the patient was given intravenous (IV) alteplase for thrombolysis. After initiation of alteplase, a CTA was performed that showed hyperperfusion in the left temporal lobe, consistent with epileptic activity. Despite halting the infusion, the patient developed an intraparenchymal hemorrhage 12 hours later. Seven days later, further investigation of the CTA showed early filling of the left transverse sinus. The patient then underwent a DSA, which showed a dAVF.A similar presentation highlighted a 66-year-old female who presented in Singapore with receptive dysphasia [13]. Head CT was negative and the patient was treated with IV alteplase. CTA was performed after the bolus, which revealed a dAVF. Despite administration of fresh frozen plasma, the patient deteriorated rapidly. Head CT showed a large intraparenchymal hematoma. The patient expired shortly later.Head CT and CTA for all suspected ischemic stroke patients has been supported in the literature [15]. However, no noninvasive imaging can provide 100% sensitivity to rule out dAVF. Noninvasive imaging findings may include cortical venous reflux, indicated by flow voids or abnormal dilatation or early enhancement of vessels [16]. One study of 108 patients found the diagnostic sensitivity of CTA in diagnosing dAVF to be 62 to 96%, while sensitivity of magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) was 58%-83% [17]. Silent MRA is a developing noncontrast enhanced method of angiography that has been proposed to better visualize cerebrovascular lesions in a noninvasive fashion [18]. Silent MRA has shown to be superior for detection of dAVF when compared to time-of-flight MRA, but it makes scanning time ten minutes longer [19].Although rare, dAVFs should remain in the differential diagnosis of brain conditions that present atypically or do not resolve with treatment as these patients are at high risk of life-threatening complications of true strokes or hemorrhage [20]. This case presentation, and others similar to it, should urge all members of the multidisciplinary team caring for patients who present with neurological symptoms to include dural arteriovenous fistula in the differential. Specifically, an intracranial lesion with nonspecific edema should raise suspicion for dAVF, as venous congestion is common [12]. DSA remains the gold standard and should be heavily considered in such cases, even in the setting of negative findings in noninvasive imaging modalities [3].

Conclusion

It is important to keep dural arteriovenous fistulas in mind when creating a differential diagnosis for a patient who presents with neurological findings, even in the absence of vascular abnormality on noninvasive imaging. Special consideration should be made when an abnormal pattern of edema is seen, which may be indicative of venous congestion. A low threshold should be maintained for the consideration of digital subtraction angiogram.

Patient consent

Written, informed consent for publication of the case was obtained from the patient.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 70-year-old male
- Chief Complaint: Left-sided weakness and dysarthria
- History of Present Illness: Initially presented with left-sided weakness and dysarthria; subsequent imaging and treatment for presumed cerebellar stroke. Two months later, presented with a fall leading to further investigation for unresolved symptoms.
- Past Medical History: Coronary artery disease, prostate cancer with prior prostatectomy, colon cancer with prior sigmoid colon resection, hypertension, previous smoking history.
- Personal History: Previous smoker.
- Family History: None mentioned.
- Physical Examination: Persistent left-sided weakness and dysarthria reported as baseline, with subjective improvement on dexamethasone treatment.
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - Initial CT: Hypodensity in right cerebellum with mass effect, reportedly consistent with cerebellar infarct. 
  - Initial MRI (FLAIR): Hyperintensity with mass effect causing hydrocephalus. Post-contrast imaging showed heterogeneous enhancement without discrete lesion.
  - CTA: Negative for vascular lesions.
  - Echocardiogram: Normal ejection fraction.
  - Subsequent imaging demonstrated persistent edema concerning for mass.
  - Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA): Revealed Borden III dural arteriovenous fistula (dAVF) between right occipital artery and posterior cerebellar vein, as well as severe right vertebral artery stenosis.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- Initial Presentation:
  1. Cerebellar Stroke: Suspected based on acute left-sided weakness/dysarthria, imaging findings of cerebellar hypodensity, and treatment initiated with aspirin and clopidogrel. However, the persistent edema and lack of clinical improvement raised doubts.
  2. Intracranial Mass: Considered after two months when CT/MRI showed unresolved edema with mass effect. However, no discrete lesion was identified on imaging.
  3. Dural Arteriovenous Fistula (dAVF): Ultimately suspected due to persistent atypical findings of edema and venous congestion. Confirmed on DSA, which showed arteriovenous shunting between the right occipital artery and posterior cerebellar vein.
  
- Rationale for Exclusions:
  - Cerebellar Stroke: Excluded after sustained diagnosis of persistent edema, lack of discrete occlusion, and findings inconsistent with ischemic pathology on further workup.
  - Intracranial Mass: Excluded due to lack of clear neoplastic features on imaging and eventual confirmation of vascular abnormality via DSA.
  
- Diagnostic Confirmation:
  - dAVF was confirmed with DSA, the gold standard for evaluation, which identified the specific arteriovenous shunting not visualized on prior noninvasive imaging.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Borden III dural arteriovenous fistula (dAVF) involving the right occipital artery and posterior cerebellar vein, presenting with venous congestion and stroke-mimic symptoms.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by going through the patient's case step by step. The patient is a 70-year-old male with left-sided weakness and dysarthria. Initially, they thought it was a cerebellar stroke because the CT showed a hypodensity in the right cerebellum with mass effect. But then, two months later, after a fall, more tests were done because the symptoms weren't resolving.

First, I need to consider the initial presentation. The symptoms of left-sided weakness and dysarthria do point towards a stroke, especially in the cerebellum given the imaging findings. But the fact that there was mass effect and hydrocephalus on MRI FLAIR is a bit unusual for a typical infarct. Also, the heterogeneous enhancement post-contrast without a discrete lesion makes me think there's something else going on. Strokes usually don't enhance that way unless it's subacute, but even then, the mass effect persisting over time is a red flag.

His past medical history includes prostate and colon cancer, both of which have been treated surgically. But cancer history raises the possibility of metastases. However, the initial imaging didn't show a discrete mass, which might argue against that. But then again, some cancers can present with more infiltrative lesions or edema without a clear mass.

The CTA was negative for vascular lesions, so maybe not a typical ischemic stroke. The echocardiogram was normal, so cardioembolic sources might be less likely. Then the DSA came back showing a Borden III dAVF between the right occipital artery and posterior cerebellar vein, along with right vertebral artery stenosis. 

Dural arteriovenous fistulas (dAVFs) can present with various neurological symptoms. Borden III indicates cortical venous drainage, which is high risk for hemorrhage or venous hypertension. Venous hypertension from the dAVF could lead to edema, mass effect, and symptoms mimicking a stroke. The persistent edema on imaging that was concerning for a mass might actually be due to the vascular abnormality causing venous congestion. 

Dexamethasone is a steroid that reduces edema. The patient's subjective improvement on dexamethasone supports the idea of edema being a contributing factor. But why did they initially think it was a stroke? Because the imaging showed hypodensity, which can be seen in infarcts, but the subsequent findings of enhancement and mass effect not resolving as expected for a stroke should have prompted further investigation.

The presence of severe right vertebral artery stenosis is another factor. Stenosis could contribute to ischemic events, but the combination with the dAVF complicates things. The dAVF's venous congestion might be the primary issue here, leading to the symptoms and imaging findings that were initially mistaken for a stroke.

Putting it all together, the key diagnosis here is the Borden III dAVF. The unresolved symptoms, imaging discrepancies, and the DSA findings all point to this as the underlying cause. The vertebral artery stenosis is a comorbidity but the main culprit for the presenting symptoms and persistent issues is likely the dAVF causing venous hypertension and edema.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient initially presented with symptoms suggestive of cerebellar stroke (left-sided weakness, dysarthria), supported by CT findings of right cerebellar hypodensity. However, persistent edema on follow-up imaging and lack of resolution raised suspicion for an alternative etiology.  
<step 2> MRI FLAIR hyperintensity with mass effect and hydrocephalus, along with heterogeneous post-contrast enhancement, is atypical for ischemic stroke. These features are more consistent with vascular malformations, tumors, or inflammatory processes. Metastasis from prostate/colon cancer was possible but not definitively identified.  
<step 3> CTA ruled out large-vessel occlusion but could not exclude smaller vascular anomalies. Subsequent DSA identified a Borden III dAVF, a high-risk lesion with cortical venous drainage, which can cause venous hypertension, edema, and mass effect mimicking stroke.  
<step 4> The patient’s transient improvement on dexamethasone aligns with reduced perilesional edema, a feature seen in dAVF-related venous congestion rather than ischemic infarcts. Severe vertebral artery stenosis, while contributory to vascular risk, does not explain the persistent imaging abnormalities.  
<step 5> Borden III dAVFs are notorious for delayed diagnosis due to overlapping symptoms with stroke. The fistula’s arterialized venous drainage into the posterior cerebellar veins likely caused venous hypertension, explaining the initial "stroke-like" presentation and subsequent hydrocephalus.  

### Answer:
Borden III dural arteriovenous fistula (dAVF) with associated venous hypertension.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient initially presented with symptoms suggestive of cerebellar stroke (left-sided weakness, dysarthria), supported by CT findings of right cerebellar hypodensity. However, persistent edema on follow-up imaging and lack of resolution raised suspicion for an alternative etiology.

MRI FLAIR hyperintensity with mass effect and hydrocephalus, along with heterogeneous post-contrast enhancement, is atypical for ischemic stroke. These features are more consistent with vascular malformations, tumors, or inflammatory processes. Metastasis from prostate/colon cancer was possible but not definitively identified.

CTA ruled out large-vessel occlusion but could not exclude smaller vascular anomalies. Subsequent DSA identified a Borden III dAVF, a high-risk lesion with cortical venous drainage, which can cause venous hypertension, edema, and mass effect mimicking stroke.

The patient’s transient improvement on dexamethasone aligns with reduced perilesional edema, a feature seen in dAVF-related venous congestion rather than ischemic infarcts. Severe vertebral artery stenosis, while contributory to vascular risk, does not explain the persistent imaging abnormalities.

Borden III dAVFs are notorious for delayed diagnosis due to overlapping symptoms with stroke. The fistula’s arterialized venous drainage into the posterior cerebellar veins likely caused venous hypertension, explaining the initial "stroke-like" presentation and subsequent hydrocephalus.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial Presentation: Cerebellar Stroke suspected based on acute left-sided weakness/dysarthria, imaging findings of cerebellar hypodensity, and treatment initiated with aspirin and clopidogrel; however, persistent edema and lack of clinical improvement raised doubts.
Intracranial Mass considered after two months when CT/MRI showed unresolved edema with mass effect but no discrete lesion identified.

Dural Arteriovenous Fistula (dAVF) ultimately suspected due to persistent atypical findings of edema and venous congestion; confirmed on DSA showing arteriovenous shunting between the right occipital artery and posterior cerebellar vein.
Cerebellar Stroke excluded after sustained diagnosis of persistent edema, lack of discrete occlusion, and findings inconsistent with ischemic pathology on further workup.
Intracranial Mass excluded due to lack of clear neoplastic features on imaging and eventual confirmation of vascular abnormality via DSA.
Diagnostic Confirmation: dAVF confirmed with DSA, the gold standard for evaluation, identifying specific arteriovenous shunting not visualized on prior noninvasive imaging.
Final Diagnosis: Borden III dural arteriovenous fistula (dAVF) involving the right occipital artery and posterior cerebellar vein, presenting with venous congestion and stroke-mimic symptoms.
